sections.title: Be careful
items: To form a question, invert the modal and the subject: **Should** we leave now?
Modals can also be used in passive structures: The form **must be signed** by a parent.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Use **be able to** when you need a tense that *can* does not have: It's great to **be able to** work from home.
Future: After more practice, you'll **be able to** speak Spanish fluently.
Present perfect: Have you **been able to** reach her on the phone?

sections.title: Be careful
items: **May** sounds more formal and polite than **could**, and **could** is more polite than **can**.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with can, could or be able to
exercises.title: A: Complete with can, could or be able to
exercises.instructions: Write the correct form in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: I enjoy swimming, but I ...... dive very well yet.
items.explanation: Not able yet → **can't** dive very well.

items.sentence: When my dad was young, he ...... climb trees easily.
items.explanation: Past ability → **could** climb trees.

items.sentence: After a few more lessons, she'll ...... ride a motorbike safely.
items.explanation: Future ability → **be able to** ride safely.

items.sentence: They haven't ...... contact the hotel yet.
items.explanation: Present perfect ability → **been able to** contact.

items.sentence: ...... you speak English when you were ten?
items.explanation: Past ability question → **Could** you speak English?

items.sentence: If we save enough money, we'll ...... buy a better computer.
items.explanation: Future with condition → **be able to** buy.

items.sentence: I'm sorry, I ...... meet you after school today.
items.explanation: Not possible today → **can't** meet you.

items.sentence: He wasn't ...... open the door because he had lost the key.
items.explanation: Past inability → wasn't **able to** open the door.

exercises.title: B: Permission (choose the correct modal)
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.sentenceAfter: I speak to the manager, please?
items.originalSentence: **Can / May** I speak to the manager, please?
items.completedSentence: May I speak to the manager, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Ability or informal permission fits better.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May is formal permission. Can is everyday permission or ability.

wrongOptions.can: Can can sound informal where a rule or polite permission is needed.
wrongOptions.may: May is formal — it may not fit informal ability sentences.
wrongOptions.Can: "Can" breaks the grammar pattern described above.

items.sentenceAfter: use my charger if your phone battery is low.
items.originalSentence: You **can / could** use my charger if your phone battery is low.
items.completedSentence: You can use my charger if your phone battery is low.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The speaker is giving permission now.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Can often gives permission or states ability. Could is more polite or past.

wrongOptions.can: Can may be too direct or present-tense for this context.
wrongOptions.could: Could may be too tentative when clear present permission is meant.

items.sentenceAfter: you help me carry this box, please?
items.originalSentence: **Could / Can** you help me carry this box, please?
items.completedSentence: Could you help me carry this box, please?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Could softens the request or refers to past ability.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Can often gives permission or states ability. Could is more polite or past.

wrongOptions.can: Can may be too direct or present-tense for this context.
wrongOptions.could: Could may be too tentative when clear present permission is meant.
wrongOptions.Can: "Can" breaks the grammar pattern described above.

items.originalSentence: Visitors **may / could** enter the museum after 10 a.m.
items.completedSentence: Visitors may enter the museum after 10 a.m.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The level of formality and meaning in the sentence matches one modal, not the other.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May states permission or possibility in formal English. Could is a polite request or past ability.

wrongOptions.could: Could does not express the same type of permission or rule here.
wrongOptions.may: May does not match the intended meaning or register here.

items.sentenceAfter: I have another copy of the form?
items.originalSentence: **May / Can** I have another copy of the form?
items.completedSentence: May I have another copy of the form?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Ability or informal permission fits better.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May is formal permission. Can is everyday permission or ability.

wrongOptions.can: Can can sound informal where a rule or polite permission is needed.
wrongOptions.may: May is formal — it may not fit informal ability sentences.
wrongOptions.Can: "Can" breaks the grammar pattern described above.

items.originalSentence: You **couldn't / can't** swim here. It's dangerous.
items.completedSentence: You can't swim here. It's dangerous.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "can't" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "can't" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "couldn't" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.couldn't: "couldn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("swim here. It's dangerous.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: you show me the way to the library?
items.originalSentence: Excuse me, **could / may** you show me the way to the library?
items.completedSentence: Excuse me, could you show me the way to the library?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The level of formality and meaning in the sentence matches one modal, not the other.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: May states permission or possibility in formal English. Could is a polite request or past ability.

wrongOptions.could: Could does not express the same type of permission or rule here.
wrongOptions.may: May does not match the intended meaning or register here.

items.originalSentence: No, you **may not / could not** take photographs inside the gallery.
items.completedSentence: No, you may not take photographs inside the gallery.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "may not" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "may not" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "could not" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.could not: "could not" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("take photographs inside the gallery.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: C: Advice (should / ought to)
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with should, shouldn't, ought to or ought not to.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with should, shouldn't, ought to or ought not to.

items.sentence: It's raining. You ...... take an umbrella.
items.explanation: Good advice → you **should** take an umbrella.

items.sentence: We ...... leave our bags unattended at the station.
items.explanation: Bad idea → **shouldn't** leave bags unattended.

items.sentence: If she wants to improve, she ...... practise every day.
items.explanation: Advice → she **ought to** practise every day.

items.sentence: You ...... spend all afternoon looking at a screen.
items.explanation: Bad idea → **ought not to** spend all afternoon on a screen.

items.sentence: What ...... we do if the train is cancelled?
items.explanation: Asking for advice → what **should** we do?

items.sentence: You ...... ask your parents before you go out.
items.explanation: Advice → you **should** ask your parents.

items.sentence: Drivers ...... use their phones while driving.
items.explanation: Bad idea → drivers **shouldn't** use phones.

items.sentence: We ...... check the address before we leave.
items.explanation: Advice → we **ought to** check the address.

exercises.title: D: Rewrite using a modal
exercises.instructions: Rewrite each sentence using the modal in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Rewrite each sentence using the modal in brackets.

items.sentence:
Is it possible for you to send me the file today? (can)
...... you send me the file today?
items.explanation: Ability/possibility → **Can** you send me the file?

items.sentence:
It is not a good idea to eat too quickly. (shouldn't)
You ...... eat too quickly.
items.explanation: Bad idea → you **shouldn't** eat too quickly.

items.sentence:
I had the ability to play chess when I was six. (could)
I ...... play chess when I was six.
items.explanation: Past ability → I **could** play chess.

items.sentence:
Is it all right if I open the door? (may)
...... I open the door?
items.explanation: Permission → **May** I open the door?

items.sentence:
It is not a good idea to leave your bike unlocked. (shouldn't)
You ...... leave your bike unlocked.
items.explanation: Bad idea → **shouldn't** leave your bike unlocked.

items.sentence:
They have managed to repair the machine. (been able to)
They have ...... repair the machine.
items.explanation: Managed to → have **been able to** repair.

exercises.title: E: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a modal verb so that it means the same as the first.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a modal verb so that it means the same as the first.

items.sentence:
It's a bad idea to walk home alone late at night.
You ...... home alone late at night.
items.explanation: Bad idea → **shouldn't walk** home alone.

items.sentence:
Is it OK if I borrow your bike?
...... your bike?
items.explanation: Permission → **May I borrow** your bike?

items.sentence:
When I was nine, I had the ability to cook simple meals.
When I was nine, I ...... .
items.explanation: Past ability → I **could cook** simple meals.

items.sentence:
Soon they will manage to finish the bridge.
Soon they will ...... finish the bridge.
items.explanation: Future ability → **be able to** finish the bridge.

items.sentence:
Do you think I should apologise to her?
Do you think I ...... to her?
items.explanation: Advice → **ought to apologise** to her.

items.sentence:
Please don't enter this room without permission.
You ...... this room without permission.
items.explanation: Prohibition → **mustn't enter** without permission.

items.sentenceBefore: 'I feel really nervous about the interview.' 'You
items.explanation: Reassurance → you **shouldn't worry** so much.
Past ability → I **could** draw realistic animals.

items.sentenceAfter: I use your ruler?' 'Yes, of course.'
items.explanation: Permission request → **May** I use your ruler?

items.sentenceAfter: take a coat because it's cold outside.
items.explanation: Advice because it's cold → **should** take a coat.

items.sentenceBefore: By the end of the course, you
items.explanation: Future ability → **will be able to** design a website.
Rule in a library → **shouldn't** talk loudly.

exercises.title: G: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one mistake with a modal. Write the correct sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one mistake with a modal. Write the correct sentence.

items.explanation: **Can** has no -s → **can** ride (not **cans**).

items.displayPrompt: You should to call your grandmother.

cues: You should to call your grandmother.

items.explanation: **Should** + base verb → **should call** (no **to**).

items.displayPrompt: May I to ask a question?

cues: May I to ask a question?

items.explanation: **May** + base verb → **May I ask** (no **to**).

items.displayPrompt: We didn't could find the address.

cues: We didn't could find the address.

items.explanation: Past negative → **couldn't** find (not **didn't could**).

items.displayPrompt: She will can join us next week.

cues: She will can join us next week.

items.answer: She will be able to join us next week.
acceptedAnswers: She will be able to join us next week.
items.explanation: Future ability → **will be able to** (not **will can**).

items.displayPrompt: They ought not arrive so late.

cues: They ought not arrive so late.

items.answer: They ought not to arrive so late.
acceptedAnswers: They ought not to arrive so late.
items.explanation: Negative advice → **ought not to** arrive.

exercises.title: H: Mixed practice
exercises.instructions: Complete each gap with one suitable modal expression.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each gap with one suitable modal expression.

items.sentence: Excuse me, ...... I sit next to you?
items.explanation: Polite permission → **may** I sit next to you?

items.sentence: You ...... drink more water when it's hot.
items.explanation: Health advice → **should** drink more water.

items.sentence: I ...... tie my shoes until I was five.
items.explanation: Past inability → **couldn't** tie my shoes.

items.sentence: With more practice, you'll ...... play this song perfectly.
items.explanation: Future ability → **be able to** play perfectly.

items.sentence: ...... you possibly speak more slowly, please?
items.explanation: Polite request → **Could** you speak more slowly?

items.sentence: We ...... book the tickets before they sell out.
items.explanation: Advice → **ought to** book the tickets.

items.sentence: Visitors ...... touch the paintings in the museum.
items.explanation: Prohibition → **mustn't** touch the paintings.

items.sentence: My cousin ...... speak Italian quite well now.
items.explanation: Present ability → **can** speak Italian now.

exercises.title: I: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
I knew how to swim at the age of six.
I ...... at the age of six.
items.explanation: Past ability → **could swim** at the age of six.

items.sentence:
It's against the rules to take photos here.
You ...... photos here.
items.explanation: Against the rules → **aren't allowed to take** photos.

items.sentence:
The dentist told me to eat fewer sweets.
The dentist said I ...... fewer sweets.
items.explanation: Reported advice → **should eat** fewer sweets.

items.sentence:
She managed to answer all the questions correctly.
She ...... all the questions correctly.
items.explanation: Succeeded → **was able to answer** all the questions.

items.sentence:
Is it OK to use dictionaries in the test?
...... dictionaries in the test?
items.explanation: Permission question → **Are we allowed to use** dictionaries?

items.sentence:
My coach says I should train more often.
My coach says I ...... more often.
items.explanation: Advice → **ought to train** more often.
